label: Creation and Cosmogony
description: Passages about dahišn, creation, the ordering of the world, and the first beings.

label: Good and Evil
description: Passages about Ohrmazd, Ahreman/Ahriman, druj, druwand, ahlaw, and cosmic opposition.

label: Soul and Afterlife
description: Passages about ruwan, gyan, judgment, reward, punishment, heaven, hell, resurrection, and final restoration.

label: Ritual and Purity
description: Passages about purity, pollution, ritual cleansing, padyab, nasay, and yojdahr.

label: Fire and Water
description: Passages where fire, water, adur/ataxsh, aban, and ritual elements are thematically important.

label: Wisdom and Knowledge
description: Passages about xrad, danishn, agahi, religious knowledge, and instruction.

label: Law and Judgment
description: Passages about law, questions and answers, recompense, sin, and juridical reasoning.

label: Ritual Worship
description: Passages about yasn/yazišn, prayer, praise, offerings, priests, and ritual performance.

label: The story of Dahāg and Fredon
description: Θraētaona’s most brilliant feat in the Avesta is his victory over the three-headed, six-eyed dragon Dahāka (Yt. 5.33-35, 15.23-24; Y. 9.7-8; Vd. 1.18). Although there and in a tradition related by Ṭabarī (I, p. 208) Θraētaona seems to have killed Dahāka, in the Pahlavi literature (Dēnkard, ed. Madan, pp. 548, 811) it is explicitly reported that, following Ohrmazd’s command, he refrained from killing the dragon, lest various noxious creatures emerge from the corpse; rather, he fettered and imprisoned the beast on Mount Damāvand (q.v.). It will remain there in chains until the end of the world, when it will be slain by Garšāsb (q.v.; Pahlavi Rivayat, ed. Dhabhar, pp. 146-47). This version is also repeated in sources of the Islamic period (Ṭabarī, I, p. 208; Šāh-nāma, ed. Khaleghi, I, pp. 82-83). It seems to be a late Zoroastrian scholastic elaboration of the original Avestan myth (Boyce, Zoroastrianism I, p. 283). It is obviously because of this victory that Ferēdūn is often given the epithets purr-pērōzgar “very victorious” (Pahlavī Rivayat, ed. Dhabhar, p. 134), tagīg “valiant,” and zōrīg “powerful” (Dēnkard, ed. Madan, II, pp. 812, 814).
